commonRoofingChallenges: HOA approval cycles routinely add 1–3 weeks to project schedules
Color/material conformity required within most subdivision covenants
Spring hail events driving heavy insurance-claim volume
Production-builder original shingles often near or past their useful life
uniqueAngleElaboration: O'Fallon, MO has been one of the fastest-growing cities in the St. Louis metro for the past two decades, and the housing stock reflects that — the overwhelming majority of homes were built between the early 1990s and today, in subdivisions designed for architectural consistency. That consistency is enforced by HOA architectural review boards, which means color changes, material changes, and even shingle-line changes typically require formal approval before a roof gets replaced. For homeowners, this is the single most important roofing-specific thing about O'Fallon: starting an HOA approval early can shave weeks off the project timeline, and choosing a contractor who's done HOA approval submissions before saves a meaningful amount of friction. Many of the original production-builder shingles are now 15–25 years old, putting a large share of O'Fallon's housing in active replacement consideration — and spring hail events are accelerating that timeline for many homeowners through insurance claims.
